references.notes: The term 'maniyya' (fate) is personified as an active force, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about fate as an irresistible power.
The verse refers to 'الدَّهْرُ' (Time/Fate) as an entity that gives counsel, reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in Dahr as a powerful, often destructive, cosmological force.
The verb "تَعَيَّفوا" refers to the practice of augury or seeking omens, specifically from animals or birds, which is a form of pre-Islamic divination.
The mention of "أَبو الفِراخِ" (a bird, often a raven) croaking ("يَنعَبُ") is a classic reference to bird augury, a pre-Islamic divinatory practice.
This verse explicitly refers to "their god, an idol," indicating the worship of idols or specific deities in pre-Islamic Arabian religion.
The phrase "birds of ill omen croak" directly describes a form of augury from birds, a common pre-Islamic divinatory practice.
The term "المَغاوِلُ" refers to ghūls, which are supernatural beings or jinn in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore, often associated with deserts and shapeshifting.

This verse explicitly mentions 'al-hām' (owls, often associated with the souls of the dead) and 'al-ṣadā' (the bone-bird, representing the soul of the deceased crying for vengeance), which are key elements of pre-Islamic mortuary beliefs.
The verse refers to 'dahr' (time/fate) as an active, destructive force that scattered people, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views of dahr.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) is used to describe an irresistible force that takes people, reflecting a pre-Islamic understanding of fate and death as cosmological agents.
The term 'السعالي' (al-saʿālī) refers to ghoul-like female jinn, which are supernatural entities in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse contains an oath sworn by Allah, a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabia where Allah was recognized as a supreme deity.
The phrase "وَجَدِّكَ" is an oath sworn by one's ancestor or fortune, a common pre-Islamic oath formula.
This verse describes the physical state of the deceased as decayed bones under planks, referring to the practice of burial.

references.entity_or_term: وُسِّدتُ حَثحَثَةً في قَعرِ مُظلِمَةِ الأَرجاءِ
references.notes: This verse further elaborates on the burial process, describing being laid in a deep, dark grave.
This verse refers to the pre-Islamic belief of the *ṣadā* or *hāmā*, where the soul of the deceased transforms into a bird (often an owl) that perches on the grave.
The term "مَلحودَةٍ" refers to a specific type of grave (a niche grave), indicating a pre-Islamic burial practice.
This verse explicitly mentions a grave (qabr) and a shroud (kafan), which are direct references to mortuary practices and beliefs about death.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) refers to an impersonal, irresistible force of destiny, a common pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The phrase 'fa-wa-llāhi' is an explicit oath by God, a common pre-Islamic oath formula.
The term 'المنون' (al-manūn) refers to fate or death as an inescapable cosmological force, a common concept in pre-Islamic thought.
The black raven is explicitly stated to have 'informed' them, indicating a belief in augury from birds, a pre-Islamic divinatory practice.
This verse personifies 'Death' (الموت) as an inescapable 'driver' (حادي) following all people, reflecting a pre-Islamic fatalistic view of death as an active, cosmological force akin to manāyā.
The term 'الصدي' (al-ṣadā) refers to a pre-Islamic belief about a bird or spirit emerging from the skull of the deceased, representing the soul or a spirit seeking vengeance.
The phrase 'لَعَمرُكَ' (by your life) is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by the life of the addressed person, which was considered a sacred entity.
The term 'المنايا' (al-manāyā) refers to fate or death as an inescapable, predetermined cosmological force, a prevalent concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'مَنِيَّتُهُ' (manīyatuhu) signifies one's appointed death or fate, emphasizing its predetermined and unavoidable nature as a cosmological force.

The phrase 'حَبلُ المَنِيَّةِ' (ḥabl al-manīya) refers to the 'rope of fate/death,' personifying death as an inevitable, binding force that ensnares individuals, a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
This verse explicitly refers to stars influencing human fate by bringing ill-fortune (anḥus) and good-fortune (suʿūd), which is a form of astral belief common in pre-Islamic cosmology.
This verse refers to 'the God' (al-Ilāh) and 'His worshipped face' as eternal, contrasting with the perishable nature of all else, indicating a belief in a supreme deity in a pre-Islamic context.
This verse explicitly names Wadd, a prominent pre-Islamic deity, particularly worshipped in South Arabia and known in other regions.
The phrase 'Dhāt al-Masājid' refers to a place containing 'masājid' (places of prostration or worship), indicating a sacred geography or sanctuary in a pre-Islamic context.
This verse describes the state of human spirits (أرواح) and bodies (أجساد) passing under the earth after death, reflecting a conceptual understanding of the afterlife.
This verse refers to the *farḍ*, a type of arrow used in pre-Islamic *maysir* (gambling with arrows), a practice often associated with divination using *azlām* (divining arrows).
This verse contains the oath formula 'لَعَمري' (by my life), which is a common pre-Islamic oath used to lend solemnity to a statement.
This verse refers to al-Anwāʾ, specific stars whose rising and setting were believed to influence weather, reflecting pre-Islamic astral belief and cosmological understanding.
The poet uses 'Allāh' in an imprecation, which in a pre-Islamic context refers to the high god of the Arabian pantheon, fitting the category of invoking a deity.
The verse attributes agency to "al-Dahr" (Time/Fate), depicting it as a force that causes separation and injustice, which is a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse explicitly mentions "al-Ghul," a type of monstrous supernatural being from pre-Islamic Arabian folklore, often associated with deserts and wilderness.
The term "sayuḥtanaṭ" refers to the preparation of the deceased for burial, specifically embalming or shrouding, which is a practice related to pre-Islamic mortuary beliefs.
The verse attributes an action (giving a choice leading to destruction) to "al-Dahr" (Time/Fate), which was considered a powerful cosmological force in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.

These verses criticize the addressee for failing to fulfill the obligation of 'al-watr' (vengeance or blood-feud), which was a crucial quasi-ritual duty in pre-Islamic Arabian society related to death and honor.
The verse invokes 'al-ilāh' (the god) to bless the water, reflecting a pre-Islamic belief in a high god capable of divine intervention and blessing.
The verse attributes the desolation of the dwellings to 'سالف الدهر' (the past of time/fate), reflecting the pre-Islamic fatalistic concept of dahr as an impersonal force.
The phrase 'سالِفِ الدَهرِ' (the past of time/fate) again invokes dahr as a significant, overarching concept of time and destiny in the pre-Islamic worldview.
The phrase 'غياهيب آجال' (depths of destinies/appointed times) refers to the hidden, predetermined nature of fate, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological concepts.
Tabāla is mentioned as a geographical location, which was a significant pre-Islamic sanctuary and cultic site, thus referencing sacred geography.
The verse explicitly mentions "السعالي", which are a type of ghoul or female jinn in pre-Islamic Arabian folklore, thus referring to supernatural beings.

The term 'al-ʿabqarī' refers to something of extraordinary quality, derived from 'Abqar', a legendary place believed to be inhabited by jinn, thus implicitly referencing supernatural beings and their craftsmanship.
This verse explicitly mentions the 'hāma,' which in pre-Islamic belief was the spirit of a murdered person, often depicted as a bird (owl) emerging from the skull, crying for vengeance until avenged.
The phrase 'ما حَمَينا' (what we have protected/made inviolable) alludes to the pre-Islamic concept of ḥimā, a sacred or protected zone.
The phrase 'لَعَمرُكَ' is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by the life of the addressee, which functions as a sacred entity in this context.
The verse explicitly refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a powerful, changing force, which is a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
